statement.markdown: Given an array of integers, find the largest element in the array. The array will contain at least one element. The function should return the largest element.

constraints.description: number of elements in the array

examples.explanation: The largest element in the array is 99.
The largest element in the array is 10.
The largest element in the array is 100.

hints.text: To find the largest element in an array, you should at least iterate through the array once to examine each element.
A common approach to finding the maximum value in a collection is to keep track of the maximum value seen so far as you iterate through the collection.

The key pattern or technique here is to initialize a variable to hold the maximum seen so far with the first element of the array, and then update this maximum whenever a larger element is encountered during iteration.

editorial.title: Finding the Largest Element in an Array
editorial.intuition: The problem requires identifying the largest element within an array of integers. A brute force approach would involve comparing each element with every other, but this is inefficient. A key observation is that we only need to keep track of the maximum element seen so far to find the largest element in a single pass.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force method would involve comparing each element with every other element in the array, which would result in a time complexity of O(n^2). This approach, while correct, is slow for large inputs because it performs redundant comparisons.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach is to iterate through the array once, keeping track of the maximum element seen so far. This can be achieved using a simple loop or, more concisely, by utilizing the built-in max function in Python.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution leverages the fact that we only need to compare each element once to find the maximum. This is achieved with a time complexity of O(n) and a space complexity of O(1), making it efficient for large inputs.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by induction. The base case is an array with one element, where the maximum is trivially that element. For an array of length n+1, if the maximum of the first n elements is known, comparing it with the (n+1)th element will yield the maximum of the entire array. Thus, iterating through the array and keeping track of the maximum seen so far will correctly find the largest element.
editorial.dryRun: For an example array [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2], initializing the maximum to the first element (3) and then iterating through the rest of the array, updating the maximum as needed (e.g., updating to 4, then 5, then 9), results in correctly identifying 9 as the largest element.
commonMistakes: Assuming a sorting approach is necessary, which would increase the time complexity unnecessarily.
Forgetting to initialize the maximum value correctly or updating it improperly during iteration.
Overcomplicating the solution by considering unnecessary comparisons or data structures.
